subject: ap-psychology
title: Sensation and Perception — Depth Cues, Memory, and Social Cognition
stimulus:
Claire's sports team made it to the championship basketball game. To play the game, the teammates will need
to work together to move a ball toward their own goal. They must then aim carefully to throw the ball into a
hoop that is elevated about 10 feet off the ground. The winner is the team that gets the ball into the hoop the
most times.

parts.prompt: Explain how Retinal disparity relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the difference in visual signals from each of Claire's eyes allows  her to see depth, which helps her be successful during the game.

required_elements: Claire can see depth because she sees two slightly different images out of each eye, making her able to throw the ball accurately.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire can see depth because she sees two slightly different images out of each eye, making her able to throw the ball accurately.

wrong_examples: Claire uses linear perspective to tell how far away the goal is. Any reference to depth perception without references to two retinal images
This will help Claire with her depth perception to make more accurate free throws.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must indicate that the difference in visual signals from each of Claire's eyes allows her to see depth, which helps her be successful during the game. Responses that reference monocular cues (e.g., linear perspective) or convergence instead of two retinal images do not earn the point. Mentioning depth perception alone without referencing two different retinal images is also insufficient.

sample_responses.response: Claire can see depth because she sees two slightly different images out of each eye, making her able to throw the ball accurately into the hoop.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies retinal disparity (two slightly different images per eye) and links it to depth perception that improves her shooting accuracy.

sample_responses.response: Claire uses linear perspective to tell how far away the goal is.
sample_responses.commentary: Linear perspective is a monocular cue, not retinal disparity. The response does not reference two retinal images.

sample_responses.response: This will help Claire with her depth perception to make more accurate free throws.
sample_responses.commentary: Mentions depth perception but does not explain the mechanism (two different retinal images). The response is too vague to earn the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how Intrinsic motivation relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
Intrinsic motivation 

Response must indicate that Claire has some specific kind of internal motivation instead of  external motivation that helps her to be successful during the basketball game.

required_elements: Claire is motivated by doing her best, so she does well in the game because of her good work ethic.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire is motivated by doing her best, so she does well in the game because of her good work ethic.

required_elements: Claire is motivated by her personal goal to be the best player in the game.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire is motivated by her personal goal to be the best player in the game.

wrong_examples: Claire wants to win the approval of her parents, so she tries hard to be successful in the game.
Claire is really energized by the presence of the crowd at the game, so she does better than she typically does at practice.
Claire is really energized by the presence of the crowd at the game, so she plays better than she has in the past when she practiced alone.
Claire's teammates help each other to perform well.
Claire does poorly because people are watching her.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify a specific internal source of motivation (e.g., personal goals, love of the game, internal satisfaction) rather than external rewards or approval. Responses that describe extrinsic motivation (parental approval, prizes, external praise) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Claire is motivated by doing her best, so she does well in the game because of her good work ethic.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies an internal motivator (doing her best, personal work ethic) rather than any external reward.

sample_responses.response: Claire is motivated by her personal goal to be the best player in the game.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — a personal goal is an internal motivator consistent with intrinsic motivation.

sample_responses.response: Claire wants to win the approval of her parents, so she tries hard to be successful in the game.
sample_responses.commentary: Parental approval is an external reward. This describes extrinsic motivation, not intrinsic.

parts.prompt: Explain how Social facilitation relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the presence of others (the crowd or opposing team) enhances Claire's performance during the championship game.

required_elements: Claire plays better in the championship game because the crowd's presence increases her arousal and motivates her to perform well.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire plays better in the championship game because the crowd's presence increases her arousal and motivates her to perform well.

required_elements: Claire's performance improves when playing in front of a large crowd compared to practicing alone.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire's performance improves when playing in front of a large crowd compared to practicing alone.

wrong_examples: Claire does poorly because people are watching her.
Claire's teammates help each other to perform well.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must indicate that Claire's performance is enhanced by the physical presence of others (crowd or audience). Responses that describe social inhibition (performing worse due to observers) do not earn the point. Responses that describe cooperation or teamwork rather than audience presence do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Claire is really energized by the presence of the crowd at the game, so she does better than she typically does at practice.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that the presence of an audience energizes Claire and improves her performance relative to practicing alone.

sample_responses.response: Claire's performance improves when playing in front of a large crowd compared to when she practiced alone.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — directly contrasts crowd presence with solo practice to illustrate the social facilitation effect.

sample_responses.response: Claire does poorly because people are watching her.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes social inhibition (impaired performance due to observation), which is the opposite of social facilitation.

sample_responses.response: Claire's teammates help each other to perform well.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes cooperation/collaboration, not the social facilitation effect caused by the mere presence of an audience.

parts.prompt: Explain how Kinesthetic sense relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Claire's sensation of the movement of her body and/or the position of her body part(s) is related to her success in the game.

required_elements: Claire's ability to know where her hands are in relation to other parts of her body will help her be successful when she dribbles or shoots.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire's ability to know where her hands are in relation to other parts of her body will help her be successful when she dribbles or shoots.

required_elements: Claire's awareness of how her hand moves as she dribbles and shoots helps her score points in the game.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire's awareness of how her hand moves as she dribbles and shoots helps her score points in the game.

wrong_examples: Claire has a great sense of balance, so she does well in the game.
Claire's kinesthetic sense allows her to control her muscles.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must explain that kinesthetic sense involves awareness of body movement and/or the position of body parts, and link this to Claire's success. Responses that refer only to balance (vestibular sense) or only to the ability to move muscles without specifying body awareness do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Claire's ability to know where her hands are in relation to other parts of her body will help her be successful when she dribbles or shoots.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies kinesthetic awareness of body part position (hands relative to body) applied to game-relevant skills.

sample_responses.response: Claire's awareness of how her hand moves as she dribbles and shoots helps her score points in the game.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — kinesthetic sense as awareness of movement (dribbling, shooting) linked to scoring.

sample_responses.response: Claire has a great sense of balance, so she does well in the game.
sample_responses.commentary: Balance is processed by the vestibular sense, not the kinesthetic sense. This confuses two different sensory systems.

sample_responses.response: Claire's kinesthetic sense allows her to control her muscles, which will help her successfully shoot the ball.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes muscle control without specifying awareness of body movement or position. The kinesthetic sense is about sensing position/movement, not controlling muscles.

parts.prompt: Claire's team won the championship game. Ten years later, the team got together for a reunion at the gym where the game was originally held. At the reunion, they discussed the championship game. Explain how Context-dependent memory relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Claire remembers more about the game because she is in the same physical location where she encoded the memory.

required_elements: Claire remembered a lot of information about the game because she was in the same gym where she made the memories.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire remembered a lot of information about the game because she was in the same gym where she made the memories.

wrong_examples: Claire remembers the context of where she played, which helped her remember.
Listening to her teammates recall the game helps Claire remember more details about the game.
Claire's joy from seeing her friends reminds her of the joy of winning the game.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must indicate that Claire remembers more because she is in the same physical location (the gym) where the memory was originally encoded. Responses that use 'context' vaguely without specifying the same physical setting, responses that refer to social cues from teammates, or responses that reference mood-congruent/state-dependent memory (emotional match) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Claire remembered a lot of information about the game because she was in the same gym where she made the memories.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that returning to the original encoding environment (the gym) improves retrieval.

sample_responses.response: Claire remembers the context of where she played, which helped her remember.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague. Does not specify that the same physical location is the retrieval cue, which is the defining feature of context-dependent memory.

sample_responses.response: Claire's joy from seeing her friends reminds her of the joy of winning the game.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes mood-congruent or state-dependent memory (emotional state match), not context-dependent memory (environmental match).

parts.prompt: Claire's team won the championship game. Ten years later, the team got together for a reunion at the gym where the game was originally held. At the reunion, they discussed the championship game. Explain how Self-serving bias relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Claire takes credit for events with positive outcomes and/or blames others for negative events in her recollection of the game.

required_elements: As Claire remembers the game, she takes credit for the team winning.
alternatives.correct_example: As Claire remembers the game, she takes credit for the team winning.

required_elements: As Claire remembers the game, she blames the referees for her fouling out of the game.
alternatives.correct_example: As Claire remembers the game, she blames the referees for her fouling out of the game.

wrong_examples: Claire only does things that help herself and not others.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must indicate that Claire attributes positive outcomes to herself (internal attribution) and negative outcomes to external factors (other people, circumstances). Responses that describe selfish behavior rather than biased attribution do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: As Claire remembers the game, she takes credit for the team winning, attributing the victory to her own skill and effort.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies self-serving bias by showing Claire making an internal attribution for a positive outcome (winning).

sample_responses.response: As Claire remembers the game, she blames the referees for her fouling out of the game.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — shows self-serving bias by attributing a negative outcome (fouling out) to an external factor (referees).

sample_responses.response: Claire only does things that help herself and not others.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes selfish behavior, not the attributional bias (credit for successes, blame for failures). Self-serving bias is a cognitive distortion in memory and attribution, not a behavioral pattern.

parts.prompt: Claire's team won the championship game. Ten years later, the team got together for a reunion at the gym where the game was originally held. At the reunion, they discussed the championship game. Explain how Retroactive interference relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that a recent memory is preventing Claire from remembering something about the championship game.

required_elements: Claire couldn't remember what number she had in the championship game because she was confusing it with her college number.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire couldn't remember what number she had in the championship game because she was confusing it with her college number.

required_elements: Claire couldn't recall the exact final score in the Championship game because she confused it with all the scores of games she had played since then.
alternatives.correct_example: Claire couldn't recall the exact final score in the Championship game because she confused it with all the scores of games she had played since then.

wrong_examples: Claire can't remember her current team members' names because she can only remember the old team members' names.
Claire interferes with how people remember retroactively, meaning she gets involved after the fact.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must indicate that newer information (memories formed after the championship game) interferes with Claire's ability to recall the original championship game. Responses that describe proactive interference (old memories blocking new ones) do not earn the point. Responses that misdefine the term do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Claire couldn't remember what number she had in the championship game because she was confusing it with her college jersey number.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly shows newer information (college jersey number) interfering with retrieval of older information (championship game number).

sample_responses.response: Claire couldn't recall the exact final score of the Championship game because she confused it with all the scores of games she had played since then.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — newer memories (scores of subsequent games) interfere with retrieval of the championship score.

sample_responses.response: Claire can't remember her current team members' names because she can only remember the old team members' names.
sample_responses.commentary: This describes proactive interference (old memories blocking new ones), which is the reverse of retroactive interference.
